Alright, it took me a second but I finally got it figured out what the issue is.

If you look at how `read_header` is used in the library, you are supposed to pass it a **file handle**, not a file name like you did. Look [here](https://github.com/mhe/pynrrd/blob/master/nrrd.py#L378)

The reason you are getting the odd error message is because there is not a check for whether the argument to `read_header` is valid. 

Here is the code you should use:
```
import nrrd
with open('atlas.nrrd', 'rb') as fh:
    nrrd.read_header(fh)
```

I definitely think there should be a check for the input argument to `read_header` and maybe some of the other functions. The other option is to modify `read_header` to open the filename if a string is given. 

Do you want to issue a pull request for this? If not, I'll submit one.

I don't think Curio has any responsibility to deal with starvation issues caused by pathological applications, no.  Specifically, the main use of Curio (and async more generally) is for I/O bound applications.  If you're writing code that isn't that, then you're probably going to experience a variety of performance issues.  I also don't think properly written applications should have their performance penalized on account of trying to accommodate every possible weird corner case.  (Full disclosure:  without measuring it, I have no idea what polling for I/O on every task schedule would do, but I have a hard time believing that it would be free for the last scenario described). 

As for threads, there are all sorts of bad things that can happen there too.   For example, CPU-bound work can't take advantage of multiple CPU cores (the GIL).   Improper use of thread synchronization primitives can cause deadlock.   Is it the responsibility of the thread library to handle all of these cases?  Not really.

Big picture:  I'm not sure there is a "one-sized fits all" policy for Curio to follow on a lot of these scheduling decisions.    As such, I'm much more interested interested in making sure that Curio provides developers with the basic tools necessary to fix such problems should it be demanded by the application at hand.  It's okay for it to have some knobs to turn.

Hello Hernan,

I wrote a prototype which I hope could lead to closing this issue. It's a very small [change](https://github.com/CalebBell/pint/commit/2dde92094a53767ecbdb67e3cb3faedf7f6d88c0) and only preliminary right now. As an example of how it can work:

```
>>> from pint import UnitRegistry
>>> import pint
>>> u = UnitRegistry()
>>> a = u.m(2)
>>> print(a)
2 meter
>>> u.cm(a)
<Quantity(200.0, 'centimeter')>
>>> print(u.cm('2 m'))
200.0 centimeter
```

There are some reasons this is a bad idea:

- Adds confusion as to how to create/convert units; 
- The majority of units cannot be supported as they're combined and not named (for example viscosity, Pascal*seconds). 

Nevertheless, many people use and abuse Python's flexibility to great advantage. This is neither an endorsement nor a criticism of this request.

I'm going to forego adding `DateTime.diff` in favor or only having `DateTime.subtract` (`subtract` supports passing a `timedelta` or `datetime` object as the first argument so `diff` would be redundant). Eventually, I'm going to add a `TimeDelta` class that will inherit from `datetime.timedelta`. Not sure exactly what the enhancement for that class will be over `timedelta`, but as a start #10 will implement a `TimeDelta.format` method that will be used to return the "time ago" string. Eventually, the `shift`, `add`, `subtract`, `__add__`, and `__sub__` methods will be modified to return `TimeDelta` in place of `datetime.timedelta`.

Thank you so much @irvinlim for pointing this out!

First of all, sorry for breaking this for you. I was unaware of the implications this would have for clients inheriting from `addict.Dict` and the test cases did not cover the issue. Please pin `addict` to a version that works for you while we figure out what's sensible here.

If I understand correctly, @MisterVladimir's test-case is so that for a subclass of  `addict.Dict`, getting a missing variable _on the subclass_ should return an instance of _that_ subclass.

Before this change setting a missing attribute on the subclass would return a `addict.Dict` instance.This  was the functionality you were depending on @irvinlim.

The problem arises when the subclass defines its own `__init__` function with custom parameters. The following line will try to call `__init__` on the class without knowing its parameters.
https://github.com/mewwts/addict/blob/cf29d47eab24a7d935cb6841d13eac686dcd6e86/addict/addict.py#L64-L65

As far as I can see it's not possible to support _both_ these usecases at the same time. On the one hand, I feel like what we just merged is the more expected functionality, however inheriting from `addict` and accepting custom parameters in `__init__` will break if you don't implement `__missing__` on your own. So it's kind of a halfway there solution.

On the other hand, this probably breaks the appeal of inheriting from `addict` in config classes and the like. 

I'm torn, but I'm open to reverting. I need to think some more about it.

Do you guys have any other thoughts on this @irvinlim and @MisterVladimir?

Thank you!

"Linux Rules \\c2\\a3" is the LDAP standard escaping format. It is just for representation and is converted to bytes when sent to the server. It is controlled by the auto_escape parameter that calls the ldap3.utils.conv.escape_filter_chars() function. This should not be used when receiving data from the server. Returned data are utf-8 encoded only if the attribute type is known to hold utf-8 encoding. Anyway you can always access the raw bytes value with the ['raw_attributes] key of the response dictionary (or the raw property of the entry).

>  I see that you also added a np.ascontiguousarray call; is that to fix this same issue? 

Precisely.  This is fundamentally a pretty simple C code, which just takes a pointer to the first element of an array, and assumes consecutive elements are just one `double` along from that in memory.  In principle, the C code could be rewritten to take a "stride", which would solve the problem without needing to copy any data.  But this way works for me.

> I'm using the anaconda version (v104.1) which doesn't seem to have the wrapper functions you recently added.

The newer version with the wrappers is [available on conda](https://anaconda.org/moble/spinsfast/files).  It's possible that a simple `conda update` would have too many dependencies on other old things you have, which would mean conda isn't selecting the newest version of `spinsfast` because it wants to keep an old `numpy`, for example.  Alternatively, "manual" installation is trivial, even within a conda environment.  Just download the code and run `python python/setup.py install` from the top directory.

(Note that that version number is just whatever [the original `spinsfast` code](http://astrophysics.physics.fsu.edu/~huffenbe/research/spinsfast/index.html) used.  I haven't changed it, because they still own the package, so different conda version will still report the same package version.)

TinyDB certainly can _store_ spatial data, but there is no meaningful way to fetch the data (e.g. TinyDB has no way of fetching all of the points within a circle) unless you're willing to write your own query functions. Some useful links include:

 - [Advanced Usage - Queries](http://tinydb.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage.html#queries)
 - [`tinydb.queries.Query`](http://tinydb.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#tinydb.queries.Query)

Although TinyDB works fine for small workloads, if you want something that is more focused on spatial data, and you want more efficient queries something like [Tile38](http://tile38.com) is good.

I am glad you are aware of the `from_` field due to collision of the keyword `from`, although that is unrelated to your problem.

`Message` is meant to convert an *incoming* dictionary (representing a message) to an object. In other words, you should construct it from a dictionary:

```python
>>> from telepot.namedtuple import Message
>>> d = {'from': {'id': 999, 'first_name': 'Nick'}}
>>> m = Message(**d)
```

In real life, you should not need to compose that dictionary yourself. You should have gotten it from the argument to message handlers, e.g. the `msg` argument to `on_message()`, etc.

One more addition to my previous mail:

At least sql server has the distinction between varchar and nvarchar. 
Varchar can contain bytestreams of arbitral encoding. However nvarchar 
is _by definition_ and so guaranteed unicode.
If you'd want to make distinctions between field types, you could choose 
to return unicode only when it's guaranteed no encoding conflict exists.
That would mean returning nvarchar and guid's as unicode regardless of 
the flag, but xml and varchars as bytestreams.

what you're facing in that scenario is that you'd need a full list of 
all field types in all db systems that are guaranteed to contain unicode.

For instance I also use Oracle for the same application and also there 
I'm facing the same challenges but I don't know if Oracle has field 
types that enforce Unicode.

For Oracle a 'complication' is that number datatypes could return long 
as long as there are no decimals. That would reduce the use of 
Decimal.decimal objects. But that's a whole different discussion.

@dagostinelli The link you provided did not suggest using a transaction, it was suggesting an explicit batch. You are limited to a certain number of parameters (2100 here when I looked) per query so how many statements you can combine will be limited by the specific insert you are doing.

It's a workaround for sure, but it was much faster than executemany when i tested it here (I only tested with a single batch of 699 insert statements with 3 parameters per insert):

```
699 rows done via executemany in 4.85 secs
699 rows done via batch in 0.36 secs
```

It would not be difficult to build up an insert as you counted parameters and send each batch as you go.
For anyone thinking of it, I would *not* recommend trying to work around parameter limits and insert values directly.

 @keitherskine's method is probably still faster but this is simpler IMO.

@PythonFu 
看了好久才看懂发了什么在哪儿显示...你想说的问题是不是这样的？

**bug情形**
* 手机端在群里发送消息
* 使用下方程序
* 打印出了自己账号的NickName

**源码**
```python
@itchat.msg_register('Text', isGroupChat=False)
def auto_reply(msg):
    try:
        print itchat.get_friends(userName=msg['FromUserName'])['NickName']
        print itchat.get_friends(userName=msg['ToUserName'])['NickName']
        print msg['Content']
    except:
        pass
```

**解答**
* 会出现这样的问题是因为手机端的消息会同步到别的客户端
* 同步形式为传送一个消息中发送人为自己接收人为群聊的消息
* 只输出自己NickName是因为接收人没有找到对应的用户字典，所以返回的是None
* 建议下次不要使用裸的try-except，如果问题复杂一些，会给debug带来巨大的不便

**修改**
* 同样的问题还会在所有其他客户端发送消息时发生
* 我的解决方案是将这些消息全部过滤，源码如下：
```python
# 在__produce_msg中增加判断
for m in l:
    if (m['FromUserName'] == self.storageClass.userName) != (
            m['ToUserName'] == self.storageClass.userName):
        continue
```

这样修改是否能够解决你的问题？

So modifying to use data= instead of json equals in ther requests.request (aka client) call allows me to send xml in the body. See line 474 of https://github.com/asyrjasalo/RESTinstance/blob/master/src/REST/keywords.py
So change: 
```       try:
            response = client(request['method'], request['url'],
                              params=request['query'],
                              json=request['body'],
                              headers=request['headers'],
                              proxies=request['proxies'],
                              cert=request['cert'],
                              timeout=tuple(request['timeout']),
                              allow_redirects=request['allowRedirects'],
                              verify=request['sslVerify'])```
To:
```       try:
            response = client(request['method'], request['url'],
                              params=request['query'],
                              data=request['body'],
                              headers=request['headers'],
                              proxies=request['proxies'],
                              cert=request['cert'],
                              timeout=tuple(request['timeout']),
                              allow_redirects=request['allowRedirects'],
                              verify=request['sslVerify'])```

### Should be source-able

You can do this with the existing format, using this command[1]:

```
set -a; source .env
```

### Single quotes not getting removed

As far as I remember I don't a decision made to follow the docker specs (it's got to know though). I think the issue with single quotes not getting remove got introduced accidentally while supporting the escaped characters in quoted values[2]. And should be fixed. It's irritating as @kevgathuku mentioned.

We have been following POSIX-shell specification as close as possible, like system variable substitution, etc.. The whitespaces not in quotes are trimmed as well.

I think there should be any major compatibility issue to fix the single quote behaviour. Let me know your thoughts, i'm happy to make the change or accept a PR.

- `AutoChoices`, that is your `CharChoices`, but that will have to class attributes that could be overridden by passing them to the constructor (so the user can simply inherit from this class to use its own function, or just pass it for a one-time usage)

  - a `value_transform` argument which is a function to convert the constant to the value. Default to `lambda const: const.lower()`

- `AutoDisplayChoices`, which is the same but without auto-managing the value, so it will accept a list of two-tuples. Same method `display_transform` as for `AutoChoices`

as far as where the packet is received and handled, all messages that are
sent to the sink are handled by the external zmq message broker, which has
to be running in a separate process. i didn't include that message broker
in the pull request except here
<https://github.com/etseidler/python-socketio/commit/a37f6d13af528349a91e4816e0084ae08ae6c75f#diff-92b0b81caf2cf586f90ab5f5a5afa89bR35>
in the comments section of the zmq manager. the notion of having a separate
zmq message broker running is parallel to how a redis server is required to
use redis as a transport. the zmq message broker happens to be a little
more light-weight.


regarding the jumpstart_subscription method, a similar operation is
performed in redis_manager, but it is abstracted away by this line
<https://github.com/miguelgrinberg/python-socketio/blob/master/socketio/redis_manager.py#L65>.
That line leads to a subscribe command that is executed inside the redis
client itself, which can be seen here
<https://github.com/andymccurdy/redis-py/blob/master/redis/client.py#L2457>.

however... after taking another look at the jumpstart_subscription code,
i'm beginning to think it may be completely unnecessary for the zmq
transport. the subscribe message is received by the broker and then sent
back out to subscribers. the message broker doesn't do anything special
with that subscription message. i'll continue to look into this.

That is not true - you have either dual core CPU or 4-core without hyperthreading if you get only 1 thread for decoding. The idea here is to limit the number of OpenMP threads to the number of physical cores - 1, so that the scheduler is not starved for resources, even if preprocess_threads variable is higher than that. We could lift that restriction and rely on user to provide the option that makes sense.

I want to put my (light)weight on it: generally programs (e.g. `gpg`, `git`) receive configuration values from the following "sources", in that order (later ones win over previous):
- program defaults (traitlet `default_value`, however they are defined),
- config-files,
- env-var,
- command-line arguments.

So far, command-line argument take precedence over config-files because they are merged on top of them.
But with the proposed changes (i.e. #157, #246) I don't see *env-vars* taking precedence over config-files.

Am i correct to worry?

In order  to avoid the stale connection issue I mentioned above, we can have then `_engine` store using a key = connection_string and value = (engine_object, process_id). The check for the engine would look something like

    pid = os.getpid()
    connection = SQLAlchemyTarget._engine_dict.get(self.connection_string)
    if (connection is not None) or (pid != connection[1]):
        # create and reset connection
        engine = sqlalchemy.create_engine(...)
        SQLAlchemyTarget._engine_dict[self.connection_string] = (engine, pid)
    return engine


Even when we use tuples as keys, the original issue of stale tuples ("conn_string", some_other_pid) will linger in the dict. This could potentially grow large in multiple processor, multiple schema use cases.

f = si.content.customFieldsManager.field

> will pipenv use that when possible to determine dependencies?

No. Pipenv is an application depedency resolution tool. When a dependency is used as a Python package by another application, it is no longer an application. Its dependencies are determined by setup.py (or setup.cfg or whatever you use to upload to PyPI). Loading dependencies from a lock file is a sure route to dependency hell, we will likely never ever do that.
It's probably [pip's invocation of setuptools](https://github.com/pypa/pip/blob/3dd4a0d1ac0508c9b2e812fb650ded6782b087df/pip/req/req_install.py#L461).

@jaraco Looking more closely at that, the problematic piece is this expression: `types.ModuleType(%(pkg)r)`, and in particular the fact it doesn't set `__spec__` and various other attributes the interpreter is expected to see post PEP 451 (as long as `__spec__` is set, the import system can fill in most of the others, but without `__spec__` some operations won't work as expected).

So in 3.5+, what you probably want to be doing is:

1. Use https://docs.python.org/3/library/importlib.html#importlib.machinery.PathFinder.find_spec to create the namespace spec based on the values of `pkg` and `pth`.
2. Use https://docs.python.org/3/library/importlib.html#importlib.util.module_from_spec to create the module based on that

I *think* the necessary parameters to replace the current types.ModuleType call in the template would look something like:

    module_from_spec(PathFinder.find_spec(%(pkg)r, [os.path.dirname(p)])

The current explicit version check would then be replaced by a check for `module_from_spec` (which only appeared in 3.5).

If you wanted to handle 3.4 as well, then the simplest approach would likely be to extract the pieces of https://hg.python.org/cpython/file/3.5/Lib/importlib/_bootstrap.py#l570 that are applicable to `_NamespaceLoader` in 3.4.

Well, first off, if developers of other libraries are declaring constraints that are needlessly conservative (strict), we need to push back on those projects and give them good feedback on how their actions are hurting the community.

But what you're suggesting is that the build system should suppress the declarations made by those projects, creating new, divergent wheels that omit those requirements. That sounds dangerous to me, because it creates new forms of a package that violate their source, creating issues that might be confusing and difficult to track down, but also because it won't work with existing wheels (it requires a new build). Rather than _building_ different versions of the wheels with altered dependencies, I recommend instead having a feature in the installer (or post-installer) that strips or relaxes the constraints.

Of course, pip already gives one way to handle this situation. If in the top-level requirements declaration (command-line or requirements.txt) declares packages and versions, those declarations will take precedence over versions declared by dependencies. In this way, pip allows for the installation of packages that violate the constraints declared by the packages and their dependencies.

Unfortunately, that doesn't provide a complete solution because (a) `pkg_resources` will validate the dependencies of all installed packages and (b) this behavior happens implicitly on `import pkg_resources`, which frequently happens at run time, especially for packages that solicit entry points. The result is a VersionConflict is raised during `import pkg_resources`.

So my first question - how is it that the pip mechanism for resolving dependencies isn't suitable? If `pip install` had an option `--always-install-latest`, essentially bypassing the version specification and installing any available version, would that serve the purpose you're seeking? Or would you want it to only replace `==` with `>=`, in which case the option might be `--always-install-latest-for-pinned-versions`?

It does seem like what you want is something that affects the install logic and not something that simply affects the declared dependencies post-install. In any case, I'm strongly inclined that this behavior should be in the installer(s) and not in the build tool(s).

Some more data, when checking how Jython handles `cls.__module__`, I changed the function to look like this:

```python
def get_unpatched_class(cls):
    """Protect against re-patching the distutils if reloaded

    Also ensures that no other distutils extension monkeypatched the distutils
    first.
    """
    print("MRO")
    print(inspect.getmro(cls))
    print("Bases")
    print(cls.__bases__)
    external_bases = []
    for cls in inspect.getmro(cls):
        print(cls.__module__)
        if not cls.__module__.startswith('setuptools'):
            external_bases.append(cls)
    print("external_bases")
    print(external_bases)
    print("---")
    base = next(iter(external_bases))
    if not base.__module__.startswith('distutils'):
        msg = "distutils has already been patched by %r" % cls
        raise AssertionError(msg)
    return base
```

Hey @jleclanche. [Invoice line items](https://stripe.com/docs/api#invoice_line_item_object) and [invoice items](https://stripe.com/docs/api#invoiceitem_object) are two different (though related) objects.

Invoice items are regular API resources, so they have their matching class in the Python library (`stripe.InvoiceItem`).

Line items are simply nested objects that only appear within invoice objects. They don't have any API methods, so we never added a class for them. If we did, it would simply be a container for the attributes returned by the API when it returns an invoice object.

Do you think it would be useful to add such a class? We do have one in our PHP library (https://github.com/stripe/stripe-php/blob/master/lib/InvoiceLineItem.php), so there's some precedent for it.

@soupault @jni Test added.

Explanation for the new test case:
Given the image `[[0, 0,3], [0.7, 1]]`, the order of edges (pixel pairs) after sorting by cost will be like:

    0   0.3
    0.7 1
    0.3 0.7
    0.3 1
    0   0.7
    0   1

If we set `scale` to 0, `sigma` to 0 and `min_size` to 2, no region will be merged before post-processing because the cost will never be smaller than 0 (scale / segment_size). To meet the `min_size` requirement, the two pairs `(0, 0.3)` and `(0.7, 1)` will be merged first since their edge costs are the smallest. After that the `min_size` is meet and no more regions need to be merged. So we will get two segments in the end, both of size two. In the old implementation, the sizes are not updated so merging keeps going on until there's only one segment left.

[This answer](http://dba.stackexchange.com/a/58246) contains the state of the art for creating the function `get_table_update_time` in PostgreSQL. We can either:

1. Have an inaccurate but simple solution using tables in the `pg_catalog`.
2. Have an accurate table of update times created by a trigger, which has the (terrible!) effect of serializing every transaction.
3. Use an external process that listens to modifications and writes them lazily to a table.
4. Extend PostgreSQL to add this functionality.

Given the alternatives, and the [relatively trivial use cases](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/search?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=get_table_update_time) we have for this function, I think alternative 1 is preferable.

Figured I'd chip in here.  It does seem that having separate id and refresh tokens is a wide-spread and somewhat standard practice for JWTs.  However, I have to say that I like how djangorestframework-jwt does it -- namely, having a single token perform double-duty as an id and refresh token.  This provides at least one of the benefits of using refresh tokens which is to allow for sliding sessions (ones that last a certain overall amount of time but can expire more quickly after some period of inactivity).  You lose the added security of sending a more powerful secret over the wire less frequently.  But how much of a risk is sending *any* token over the wire really, so long as you're using properly configured TLS?  As far as I understand, the only way such a connection could be compromised is if someone cracked it (impossible with current tech) or you are man-in-the-middled (unlikely).

Anyone care to comment on this?

1. Location of pickling tests -- do you prefer to have them all in one place, or spread them around? 
   I see both conventions elsewhere in this library. It looks like simple tests are inline with class definitions, and high level tests are all in their own module. I think it makes sense to keep these by themselves, but just wanted to check.
2. I switched away from using tuples to represent state and am using a dict instead.  IMO this will allow better inter-version compatability and is overall safer.
3. I thought about versioning a bit. As I understand it, there are two pieces to think about:
   * At runtime: object gets pickled in one version and unpickled in a different version: 
        * As long as all the expected data is available, this should work fine. 
        * Otherwise, this can be handled by the unpickler on a case-by-case basis.
        * For example, in my implementation this will be extremely verbose, and give either an error or a warning depending on what's missing.
        * And each version gets to decide for itself what data is required vs optional in order to unpickle.
        * I don't think it actually needs to be this verbose for EvalFactor, just wanted to show the way I was thinking about it.
   * At test time: how will we know what whether code changes make it impossible to unpickle older versions, or whether an old version unpickles to something different than we expect?
        * I solved this by just storing a version-by-version history of what each object looks like pickled.
        * This ensures that we'll catch when new versions stop being able to unpickle something that was pickled by an older version.
        * And if we decide to stop supporting an older version, we can just delete it from the history (and add a descriptive message to the unpickling function).
        * There may be a better place to store this data than just inline in the file, but I couldn't think of a good way to do it. Suggestions welcome.
        * Similarly, I'm just generating it now by running it locally and copying it into the file. Again, there may be a better way to do it.
        * This may get cumbersome to maintain, so I'm open to talking about it more.
4. Git history -- I want to acknowledge the work that chrish put in and I did end up using some of his code. So are you okay merging those partial, work in progress commits? Or would you prefer I clean up that history?

I think that covers most of the concerns. Does this approach work for you? Any other ideas for how to do this?

I would certainly find this very useful, but it may be surprisingly difficult to implement in practice. Note the inconsistency even within individual geocoders' results (e.g. Nominatim's 3rd result in your example having one more field than the others). That gets worse between countries because addressing schemes vary so wildly - try some examples in China, Russia or Colombia to get a sense of how complex even building numbering schemes can become.

Mapzen's [Libpostal](https://mapzen.com/blog/inside-libpostal/) makes a valiant attempt at parsing address strings like this, but I've had mixed results with it, particularly in Colombia. I advise playing with that first and seeing whether you can get reliable, consistent output across geocoders and countries. If you can, then perhaps it's worth writing a wrapper that feeds geopy output into it. But I would definitely caution against starting from scratch on this problem.

foo()

I have a question related to this branch: would it be reasonable to use `__repr__` to implement `__eq__` by value instead of python's default which is to implement `__eq__` by testing if the two vars are the same object?  (Maybe `__hash__` too?)

For example, currently this is the situation:
```
import galsim
a = galsim.Gaussian(fwhm=0.5)
b = galsim.Gaussian(fwhm=0.5)
print a == b # returns False
print a is b # returns False
print repr(a) == repr(b) # returns True
```

We could override `__eq__` to turn the first comparison into a True, and still let the `is` operator handle the case where you really do want to check if the vars are the same object.  I've wanted this feature a few times in the past for caching results.

>Well, we do rely on the numpy printing options to make a reasonable Image repr. Does importing astropy make an Image repr blow up the screen on that machine? Based on your above snippet, it seems like it might. In which case, I think the solution may be to not rely on numpy print options at all and roll our own truncation for the Image repr.

It looks like the astropy hijacking defaults are probably the same as the numpy defaults.  So the bug is more like you *couldn't* explode the screen even if you really wanted to.  What I meant by saying we don't use the numpy printoptions outside of the unit tests is that we never even temporarily _write_ to the printoptions parameters; we do, as you point out, implicitly read them for the image reprs.

Here are 3 possible options:

1. Proceed with numpy.set_printoptions, which lets the user decide the desired level of verbosity, but has a bug with some versions of astropy.
2. Hard code an array truncation.
3. Develop galsim.set_printoptions so users could still decide verbosity.

I think I'm slightly in favor of (1) for now.

pyral does support obtaining all Milestone values in a workspace.  Milestone is a "subclass" of WorkspaceDomainObject and as such is more like a Tag or a Release entity than fields customarily thought of as a field with allowed values like Severity or Priority.  The simplest way to retrieve all the Milestone values is with a rally.get('Milestone', fetch="Name") much like your code example but without resorting to reaching inside the pyral implementation for context and session and looking for specific implementation codes and dict keys.  I've suppled a get_milestones.py script in the examples directory with the release of 1.2.2.   TLDR, In the early part of 2016, the WSAPI expanded the set of entity fields that provided an allowedValues endpoint.  Many of these fields whose type is STRING (not STATE or RATING) end up returning a boolean true value, which I will agree seems strange and discordant compared with the STATE and RATING type fields.  But, that's what is provided at this time.  For Artifacts, there appear to be only a very few STRING fields that provide intuitively sensical allowedValues results: for Defect (TestCaseStatus, TaskStatus, Package), for Story (TestCaseStatus, DefectStatus, TaskStatus, Package), for Task (State), for TestCase (DefectStatus, LastVerdict, Package, Type, Method).  Hope this helps.

After mulling it over for a few days, I think we should add any built in support for partial failures just yet.  The rationale being:

* If you're willing to trade a higher request count, you can just set your batch size to 1.  Arguably this should be the default in chalice, it's the default in the lambda API (though the console defaults to 10).  Then the whole issue of partial failures goes away.
* If you have a batch size > 1, you can always just delete the messages yourself as you process them.  This is essentially what chalice would be doing for you.  To help, I think we should map the receipt handle as an attribute on the `SQSRecord` so you can just say `sqs.delete_message(record.receipt_handle)`.
* We can add this later in a backwards compatible way.  Similar to what we did with the `Response` class for API gateway, we can eventually support some sort of `SQSResponse` that has the list of success/failures if we decide to support this.

The image data produced by imageio *is* a numpy array. It is a subclass, so that meta data can be attached, but other than that it is a normal array. You can turn it into a regular array using e.g. `np.asarray(im)`.

That said, I understand the confusion, and you're not the first to mention this. This topic is mentioned [here in the docs](http://imageio.readthedocs.io/en/stable/scipy.html), but maybe we should mention it in more places.

I've also considered making the result look more like a numpy array. E.g. by overloading ``__repr__``, but that may be fragile, and maybe it's also wrong, because that would mean we're hiding the fact that it is a subclass...

JPMML-SkLearn version 1.2.0 added limited support for the `sklearn.pipeline.Pipeline` estimator type.

By definition, the pipeline contains a list of zero or more transformation steps followed by a final estimator step. You cannot do arbitrary feature transformation work there (as it should be encapsulated into the `DataFrameMapper` object). But you can do feature selection work. For example, selecting a subset of "best" features:
```Python
pipeline = Pipeline([
  ("selector", SelectKBest(k = 5)),
  ("estimator", ...)
])

sklearn2pmml(pipeline, mapper, "Workflow.pmml")
```

Hello,

Thanks for reaching out! The warning message you're reporting is not a bug; it's an intentional message we have built into the library to suggest that your locale settings may be problematic when working with this client library. That said, it will otherwise have no effect if you choose to continue using the same configuration.

The history behind this decision is that we received several reports of encoding / decoding related errors in the past, usually when users unexpectedly received utf-8 characters in the response for the first time. The underlying cause of these issues was largely related to their locale configuration, which was typically set to ascii. I am not very familiar with cp936 encoding, but provided that it only covers a limited subset of unicode characters as ascii does, I expect you would encounter similar issues when working with incompatible unicode characters.

If you want this warning to stop appearing when initializing the client library, I recommend changing your locale configuration. This process can differ depending on your platform; for Windows, you may want to read [this article](https://answers.microsoft.com/en-us/surface/forum/surfbook-surfaccess/how-to-change-system-locale-in-windows-10-the/d7826ef0-13cd-43ea-8e01-2846d813dfed).

Regards,
Mark

What is in 4.0 seems fine: not using `ETC`, preferring folder. So, it should be OK to use `--folder /etc/exabgp` everywhere (in packaging I mean).

Also, I don't have any problem with `/etc/exabgp`. It justs that in 3.4, if you set `ETC` to `/etc/exabgp`, it is like having `--folder /etc/exabgp/exabgp` and `--env /etc/exabgp/exabgp/exabgp.env`. However, at some other places, `ETC` is used as the relative base for processes, so if we set it to `/etc`, we have `--folder /etc/exabgp`, `--env /etc/exabgp/exabgp.env` (good) but processes are searched in `/etc` (bad).

Using `--folder` is OK because we get `--env /etc/exabgp/exabgp.env` and processes are searched in `/etc/exabgp` (because `ETC` is set to `folder`).

2) Make sure your ```HTTPJSONCollector.conf``` exists at that path
3) Now make a copy of that config file and rename it to ```HTTPJSONCollector 2.conf``` (NOTE the space).
4) Make change to the new config file and then start diamond. You should now have two instances of the collector running, each using a different config.

Thanks for your response @MaxBenChrist 

> From my point of view, you showed in your benchmark that it is possible to speed up the feature calculation for two, very specific features.
> I am curious to see if the calculation of the other features can be speed up similarly.

Well those two were somewhat randomly chosen. The main difference is between doing an operation on a 2D array vs a for loop on 1D arrays. I imagine in some cases, the former would be faster due to better vectorization in BLAS used by numpy. Though, currently that wouldn't directly work for models that require `statsmodels` as the corresponding functions only support 1D arrays.

>  I have not yet worked with xarrays.

Well xarray was used mostly for convenience (to keeps labels associated with arrays dimensions), the same could be done just with numpy.arrays. 

>  tsfresh also contains logic around the extraction that eat up runtime. So, it is natural that tsfresh will show a longer runtime

Yes, and the idea could be to do the corresponding validation etc once per dataset instead of for each 1D array. But I have not looked how this could work with e.g. feature selection in your pipeline.

> this implies that all time series in the dataset have the same length, right?

Not necessarily. It implies that all time series are sampled on the same time grid. Ideally it would also be uniformly sampled ([tsfresh also makes this assumption in a few places](https://github.com/blue-yonder/tsfresh/search?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=uniformly+sampled&type=)). To handle series of different length one would need to handle missing values (e.g. via NaNs) in the feature extraction metrics.

Anyway it's just some thoughts, I'm not sure this would make sense in the general use case of tslearn..

> "strict" channel priority sounds better

IMO that is the only long term solution. Conda-forge can, and will, provide a no-features `numpy` but we are not given the freedom to do that whenever we can due to the brokenness of the state.

There are a number of other possible solutions, like reverting this behavior, then again it takes AnacondaInc freedom to roll this feature into conda.

From my point of view the only way for the two, or more, ecosystems to evolve together and benefit from each other would be the "strict" channel priority.

And just to be clear what I mean by strict channel priority means that conda will solve for the packages on the higher order channel **only** and use the lower order if, **and only if**, the packages requested are not available on the higher order channel.

I actually advocate for two more behaviors to be present on conda: the current one and a "package version priority" one. Of course all of those are not ensured to result in a stable environment but the user had the choice and will know how to debug their env under each different scenarios. (IMO only the strict one is easy to debug though.)

Pycosat was only slow once you added in those thousands of clauses from the optimization equation. With just the package dependencies, even unpruned, it could give a single SAT or UNSAT very quickly. I have given your proposal more thought, @ukoethe. As far as compilers go, this seems like a nice strategy. It would be really nice to get features out of the version strings, but you have said as much.

Personally, I still think features are somewhat limited when it comes to BLAS selection for instance ( https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1476 ). The current notion of features views them as tacked on to an essentially bare recipes. It also requires the feature package be installed before installing packages that are tied to it. This becomes problematic if a package requires a dependency that tracks the feature. The resolution currently fails in this case. I wonder if this problem would surface on Windows when having a package that requires a particular version of Python.

I have this issue as well, and it looks the same as #1930 to me.

Unsatisfiable errors mean you are trying to put packages into an environment that are inconsistent with that environment.  In this case, it looks like you have an `argparse` package in your environment, which is not compatible with python 3.6 (since argparse is *part* of python 3.6).

The likely solution is either to remove argparse, then proceed with your update.  Another option is to start using conda environments, where fewer and more purpose-specific packages mean a less likely chance for conflicts.

you can add genes to _individuals_ panels using the interface - for example for IEM:

http://www.clinicalgenomics.se/scout/user-guide/panels.html

It's fine to submit an Excel file if the headers and named properly and cells are already formatted :-)

@cmarqu Time to revisit this and see if it can be closed. I wasn't very pleased with the solutions presented so far so I did some additional thinking. @kraigher You've only expressed performance concerns and can save some time by jumping to the final paragraph.

I think the on- and off-line use cases/requirements/concerns I've heard can be summarized like this.

1. Use cases:
	* Pass messages can be used to see what requirements that are fulfilled by the DUT - I think the test cases are much better suited for this and we already have pass/fail status for those.
	* Pass messages can be used as a quality metric to show the amount of testing actually performed - Maybe a bit stronger use case than the first but we can already read the number of checks being performed and that would give a similar but much more dense metric.
	* Pass messages can be used as a simple way to provide extra debug information about the state of a DUT prior to an error. This is the best use case according to me and puts focus on presenting whatever DUT context that the check is aware of.
2. It should be possible to enable this for an existing testbench without having to rewrite the checks already written.
3. The messages should read nicely.
4. The performance impact for those not using this feature must be insignificant.

Today a failing `check(some_boolean_expression)` results in

`ERROR: Check failed.`

We actually have an exclamation mark today but I rather have a period. We have other means to put emphasis on errors so there's no need to shout.

Just adding a new `PASS` level would result in

`PASS: Check failed.`

but that would violate requirement 3. We've discussed using a more suitable default message that reads well in both situations, for example

`ERROR: Checking boolean expression.`
`PASS: Checking boolean expression.`

That works because of the `level` log format but it wouldn't read nicely with `raw`. Most people would use `level` but I still think this dependency has a bad smell. I would prefer if the default "message" is a special tag, currently `"+."` for reasons that I will explain later. It's then up to the check to decide what to do with that but if we do

`ERROR: Check failed.`
`PASS: Check passed.`

we stay readable for all formats and we also maintain the current look and feel for default error messages.

Specifying a custom message would replace the default so `check(parity_ok, "Checking parity.")` would result in

`ERROR: Checking parity.`
`PASS: Checking parity.`

The way I think about the `+` in the default message is that it tells the check to add a prefix to the rest of the message so `check(parity_ok, "+ for parity.")` would result in

`ERROR: Check failed for parity.`
`PASS: Check passed for parity.`

This means that you can also create a custom message that reads well with the `raw` format.

Today a `check_equal(1, 0, "Checking my data.")` results in

`ERROR: Equality check failed! Got 1. Expected 0. Checking my data.`

but that is inconsistent with what been described above. I would like to change this such that the first sentence is the default that is replaced if a message is given by the user. So

`check_equal(1, 0)` gives `ERROR: Equality check failed - Got 1. Expected 0.`
`check_equal(1, 0, "Checking my data")` gives `ERROR: Checking my data - Got 1. Expected 0.`
`check_equal(1, 0, "+ for my data")` gives `ERROR: Equality check failed for my data - Got 1. Expected 0.`

I use `-` to put some extra emphasis on the context which was the focus for the primary use case. In case of a passing check the context can be reduced

`PASS: Equality check passed for my data - Got 1.`

In general the check message format is

\<prefix\>\<message\> - \<context\>

where each field may or may not exist depending on the situation.

I've made the changes described above for `check_equal` and performed some performance testing with our supported simulators. My test is based on checking the output of a 32-bit counter. That is a design with very much checking in relation to design size so the relative performance hit is not likely to be as high in reality. Three of the tested simulators had a penalty between -1.3% (performance gain) to 1.5% so it seems the penalty is insignificant to the variation in simulation times. The fourth simulator had a penalty of 3.5% corresponding to 2 extra seconds added to a 54 second run with 100 million checks. Where I work 100 million checks corresponds to ~100 frames of video processing which would take days or weeks to simulate. I don't think this is a problem.

What do you think? I this a reasonable approach?

> For example, how will something like `validate(partial=True, context={partial=False})` behave? (I suppose the context will be overridden by the parameter?)

Taken literally, that form (a user-provided dict as `context`) no longer exists, since with the addition of the `app_data` parameter everything can now be specified as regular arguments. In fact, the `context` parameter should be removed altogether from the likes of `Model.validate()` that make up the main API.

Other than that, `context` always overrides any individual arguments, since the arguments could just be random defaults.

This now makes things very simple: up until the first invocation of `import_loop` or `export_loop` there is no context object — everything's in the parameters. Once we get to the loop function, it creates the context object if there was none, and from there on only `context` matters.

The only case where a context object needs to be created from scratch is when writing tests for a method that needs one:

  ```python
  mls = MultilingualStringType(default_locale='en_US')

  assert mls.to_primitive(
      {'en_US': 'snake', 'fr_FR': 'serpent'},
      context=ExportContext(app_data={'locale': 'fr_FR'})) == 'serpent'
              ^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  ```

Ok, I think I know where your problems are coming from. First, when you invoke the tox integration tests, you do need to specify the configuration section that should be used for the client. In your example above, that would be the `[client]` section. That section tells the test suite what inputs to use when creating the client, which then allows the client to connect to the server you're testing against. For the tox integration tests, the configuration section *must* be defined in a `pykmip.conf` configuration file (see the README for more info). Here you have it in your `server.conf` file, which means it won't be read properly by tox.

Second, you need to read up on server and client certificates, how they work, and how to generate them. I recommend checking out the OpenSSL documentation and tutorials, they should point you in the right direction. Throw out the `httplib2` certificate you're using; it will not help you here. Once you've read up on OpenSSL and certificates, you should be able to use OpenSSL to generate your own server certificate and with it create and sign your own client certificate. Once you have both of those certificates, use them and their keys in your client and server configs above and you should be good to go.

When you have this configured properly, you should be able to run the following command and run the integration tests:
```
tox -e integration -- --config client
```

For modifying sessions in your tests you're expected to use [session_transaction()](http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.12/testing/#accessing-and-modifying-sessions):

```
...

def test_home(client):
    with client.session_transaction() as session:
        session['message'] = 'something'
    response = client.get('/')
    assert response.data == 'something'
```

However, oddly enough, it appears that for values set in the view function you'll have to do the same, so it's not clear to me whether this is a bug with Flask or pytest-flask:
```
...

@myapp.route('/')
def home():
    session['ping'] = 'pong'
    return 'hello world'

def test_home(client):
    response = client.get('/')

    with client.session_transaction() as session:
        assert session['ping'] == 'pong' # This works

    assert session['ping'] == ['pong'] # This doesn't work (session is empty)
```
@nicoddemus thanks for pursuing the effort! (I'm the author of the  initial PoC commented by @hpk42 on which this is based, but I must admit I haven't found time to improve it myself).

Happy to have so fast an answer. Yes, the problem of having a different space for each process to live on is a tough one, and parallelizing the computation comes at the cost of a greater complexiy for handling the process, as far I understand.

First of all, I would like to address each of the problem you mention, because answering each of them can be of a great help to solve the overall problem. 

First of all, concerning the ability to serialize and to large objects between processes, I agree with you, in my experience, it is hard to serialize complex objects with pickle.

Of the second problem, keeping a synchronized state between worker, I will tell more thereafter.

And for the third problem, yes, indeed, the more direct-and-obvious approach to have global session object is to use a system lock at the operating system level, regardless of the programming language.

For your point 1 and 2, I want to adress some part of the problem and I think it would not appear a so big difficulty. The use case of spawning large fixture object is usually not to share big collections of references (aka very big objects), but rather to prepare the underlying system to be in a specific case. We could include for example:

- spawning many containerized application for a large micro-services software
- setting up a database in a pristine state with a clean table structure
- Gathering a big set of data on the local storage drive from a collection of network connections
- etc.

So here the problem is not so much to share a big object between process but to ensure that the fixture preparation process is run only once from a single process. The synchronization between processes of such objects is not really important, because basically these are objects that are precisely meant to be accessed in a concurrent fashion (webserver, database, etc).

From this point of view, I think (but maybe I am wrong) that the xdist initialization step (collection of test and the preparation of the test sessions with distribution of tests amongst runner) is especially well suited to perform such a task.

So maybe a good compromise would be to have a special xdist marker for such fixtures that would care about the initialization process. As for the return value of such fixtures, it would be probably best to enforce for such special fixtures that they return at first call an immutable primitive object (eg a string or at max a tuple of strings) that would be kept in memory by xdist.

Does it seem to you a good idea ? And does it seem to you something doable in practice given the current xdist codebase ?

There's a few things at play here which I agree makes this more difficult than it ideally could (should) be. For what it's worth, these are things we're actively looking to improve in .NET Core 3.0 and beyond, but let me try to explain the moving pieces as they are now.

As Peter said, there are two locations being flagged here by the scan for contain vulnerable pieces: the "runtime store", and the NuGet Fallback Folder. They're both used for different things at different times, but by themselves don't necessarily indicate that apps are using those versions and are thus vulnerable. Furthermore, how these things are used is different between .NET Core 1.x, 2.0, and 2.1 projects. It's different again if your ASP.NET Core application is targeting and thus running on .NET Framework, rather than on .NET Core.

Of these two locations, only the "runtime store" is tracked by installers, such that when you uninstall the files go away. The NuGet Fallback Folder is not removed when you uninstall .NET Core SDK versions and so older files will remain there until they're removed manually. The other important difference is that the "runtime store" is used by deployed applications *at runtime* (as they name implies), whereas the NuGet Fallback Folder is only used by projects during development. Once the project is published (and thus becomes an application) it either takes the assemblies it needs with it, or gets them from the "runtime store" (in 2.0) or a "shared framework" (in 2.1 and beyond).

In .NET Core 1.x and all .NET Framework projects, ASP.NET Core assemblies come from NuGet packages and are published with your application. The version you get is determined by the version of the package you reference in the project. So to ensure your project is up to date, you need to update the package versions referenced in the csproj file, then build/publish and redeploy it.

In .NET Core 2.0 projects, the ASP.NET Core assemblies come from NuGet packages at design/compile time, but typically *aren't* published with your application. Instead, at runtime they come from the "runtime store". However, the version the application needs is still dictated by the package version specified in the project file, so it's the same process for ensuring the project is up to date: update the package versions referenced in the csproj file, then build/publish and redeploy it. It also requires the additional step of ensuring the "runtime store" update is installed on the machines where the deployed application will actually run (i.e. the server).

In .NET Core 2.1 projects, the ASP.NET Core assemblies still come from NuGet packages at design/compile time, and they still aren't published with your application, but at runtime they come from a "shared framework" (located under C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.AspNetCore.App). The key difference with this is that you don't need to change the version of ASP.NET Core in the project file, but rather you can leave it completely blank (e.g. <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.AspNetCore.App" />) . The build process will then transparently set that to the "baseline" version (currently 2.1.1) which is the same version you'll find in the NuGet Fallback Folder (so that the restore can complete without going online). However, at runtime, the application will roll-forward onto the latest patch version of the shared framework it finds on the machine (currently 2.1.2). This means that as patches are released, the NuGet Fallback Folder doesn't need new packages (as the baseline stays at 2.1.1), you just need to get a new shared framework (which of course is included in the updated SDKs). It also means on the servers, you simply need to install patched shared frameworks as they're released (2.1.2, 2.1.3, 2.1.4, etc.) and *all* apps on that server that were compiled against 2.1.0 will automatically roll forward onto the patched runtime when they next restart (rather than having to have their project files updated, build/published, and redeployed).

The end result of all this is that vulnerable assemblies will continue to be found in the NuGet Fallback Folder for the life of 2.x, but that doesn't mean they're necessarily used at runtime by your applications. Scanning of machines that install the SDK will continue to show these results, but scanning of servers (where you typically only install runtime assets) should be more inline with what one would expect.

I hope that helps.

If you want to help tackle this issue, you may dig in the OpenAPI 2 and 3 specs to spot differences, or maybe find a "what's new ?" or "migration howto" like this:

https://www.blazemeter.com/blog/whats-new-in-swagger-open-api3

Here's what I noticed, already.

#### Polymorphism

The use of `discriminator` has been clarified:

https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/3.0.0/versions/3.0.0.md#composition-and-inheritance-polymorphism

(see [this commit](https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/issues/403).)

I don't think this is a breaking change. AFAIK, there was nothing about this in apispec and anyway, v3 is backwards compatible with v2 IIUC.

#### Parameter locations

https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/3.0.0/versions/3.0.0.md#parameter-locations

> Possible values are "query", "header", "path" or "cookie".

It differs slightly from OpenAPi v2:

https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/3.0.0/versions/2.0.md#fixed-fields-7

> Possible values are "query", "header", "path", "formData" or "body".

This may have an impact on `__location_map__` in [marshmallow swagger extension](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/blob/38e3cd481c3d4d903fc69ff3d048e798bb9284ae/apispec/ext/marshmallow/swagger.py#L581), which looks like a mix of the two, already:

```python
__location_map__ = {
    'query': 'query',
    'querystring': 'query',
    'json': 'body',
    'headers': 'header',
    'cookies': 'cookie',
    'form': 'formData',
    'files': 'formData',
}
```

It seems, though, that it is not just about updating that dictionary. From [the link above](https://www.blazemeter.com/blog/whats-new-in-swagger-open-api3):

> The new 'requestBody' attribute supersedes the previous 'formData' parameter type and 'body' attribute and is now more flexible in allowing the specification of different types of content with various media types.
> 
> [...]
> 
> Examples...

- We could be permissive: make apispec compatible with both v2 and v3 and let the user be responsible for the consistency.

- Or we could use a config parameter fo v2 vs. v3 and try to prevent writing v2 specs when v3 is selected and conversely.

A comprehensive list of changes would help. Contributions welcome.

Thanks @hakjoon for the clarification.

I've always been confused by `load_from`, `dump_to` and `attribute`, thinking they must overlap, somehow. Now, I think I get it.

In the general case, dump/load is symmetric and if you have a model field that is exposed with another name, you use the API name in the schema, and specify the model name as `attribute`.

```python
         class MySchema(Schema):
             ApiName = fields.String(attribute='model_name')
```

`dump_to` and `load_from` introduce asymmetry, allowing to specify a different key for dump and load:

```python
         class MySchema(Schema):
             model_name = fields.String(load_from='api_load_name', dump_to='api_dump_name')
```

If you wanted to reproduce use case 1 using these, you would need to specify both `load_from` and `dump_to` and make sure they match:

```python
         class MySchema(Schema):
             model_name = fields.String(load_from='api_name', dump_to='api_name')
```

As you're saying, this may be a bit confusing. There might be use cases where asymmetry is desirable. I can't tell.

The use case I'm familiar with is an API exposing an object model, documented with apispec. In this case, it is probably a better idea to use two different schemas. In fact, AFAIU, apispec itself doesn't know what to do when `dump_to != dump_load` so it gives up and exposes the field name (see https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/blob/dev/apispec/ext/marshmallow/swagger.py#L95 and https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/blob/dev/apispec/ext/marshmallow/swagger.py#L407).

I don't have a strong stand against `load_from` / `dump_to` (I just don't use them) and maybe my confusion came from not reading the docs thoroughly. Of course, if there is consensus that they provide a feature that is best achieved another way (using different dump/load schemas), then I guess we might as well simplify things and remove them.

Confirmed for `haydavies` and `reindl`. For `klucher`, the term involving `cos_tt` is squared so it has an erroneous effect but increases diffuse irradiance rather than decreasing.  The other sky diffuse models (`isotropic` and `king`) don't use a `cos_tt` term and don't specifically account for circumsolar irradiance.

This is an implementation error. #432 is relevant. We could either

1. fix in each sky diffuse implementation with `np.where(cos_tt<0)` or similar, or
2. change the AOI calculations to return 0 (or nan) when the sun is behind the array plane.

I'm inclined to the first option because I prefer the mathematically clean definition of AOI as the angle between the sun vector and the front-facing normal to the array, and I anticipate using AOI calculations with bifacial modeling.  In PVLib for Matlab, we implemented the first option.

If I'm reading the PR correctly, it attempts to first login plaintext, and only if that fails attempts the hashed version.

This default behaviour means that it may be be sending plaintext passwords on unsecured connections, even if using the old API where that wasn't necessary.

Even if the ordering is changed to try hashed first, it seems that it might still attempt sending plaintext e.g. if incorrect credentials specified.

I feel it would be a more secure default behaviour to never send the plaintext password unless:

a) the caller has specifically requested it; or
b) if SSL is in use it might be acceptable to try it automatically

It seems the best solution (rather than auto-detection) would be for RouterOS to send something prior to the login attempt that will indicate what login method it is expecting (possibly by means of an initial version query command, or by the welcome banner if one exists) - I've not gone down into the nitty-gritty of the API due to the appalling documentation, so I don't know which is possible. Perhaps someone who knows it in more detail can submit a request to Mikrotik support, as a fix may still be possible while it is still only a RC?

But still I don't believe this API client should ever send the password plaintext over non-SSL without the caller's explicit request to do so.

I confirm the problem, which is that Z3's vector cannot be used with std::string.
gcc's string now implements the small-string optimization, which saves a memory allocations for strings up to 15 bytes. In this case, the string object keeps a pointer to its small buffer.
When doing Z3's vector does realloc, the string objects may be moved to a different memory location, while the pointers to their small buffers are kept unchanged.
The fix should be to use std::vector instead of Z3's vector, since it knows how to properly move strings around (and Z3's datastructures don't).

Definitively something going on between memoryview, buffer, pyzmq and msgpack. I tried working around it but this obviously doesn't work in your case.

My understanding so far is:
 - pyzmq
  - cython code uses buffer on python<2.7 and memoryview on python>=2.7
  - cffi code was buggy for some times but should use the same rules. cffi is mainly for pypy, but I suspect it is used as a fallback when it fails to build using cython.
 - msgpack
  - cython version expects str (of bytes) for python<2.7 but accepts a memoryview on python>=2.7
  - if cython fails to build it uses a pure python fallback. which doesn't handle memory view, only str.
 - zerorpc
  - if python <2.7, converts pyzmq buffer to str[], for python>=2.7 expect pyzmq frame to yield a memoryview.

zerorpc doesn't handle the cases when pyzqm and msgpack are built without using cypython, which seems to be your case.

The workaround rules are complex, and sometimes even buggy on previous version of pyzmq/msgpack/zerorpc.

I will review them again, and try to come up with something smarter this time.

Hey, thanks for asking.

For work-list optimization, I was considering implementing a DFS based CFG traversal, instead of the current naïve traversal algorithm. DFS can reduce the number of iterations that required to come to the fix-point.

About parallelization, here are my two cents:

As I see it, parallelizing the algorithm should be doable. Obstacles for parallelization are mainly in implementation (Python doesn't have real multi-threading support, and multi-processing can make the code unnecessarily difficult to maintain). There should be a noticeable performance gain as long as elements in work-list are "properly" distributed to individual workers. By saying "properly", I'm talking about the dependence between analyzing individual basic blocks. For example, analysis of a basic block `B` requires the analysis result of its predecessor `A`, and if `A` and `B` are distributed onto two different workers, analysis of `B` will have to be stalled until `B` is analyzed. Fortunately, the CFG (or rather, VFG for `vsa_ddg`) is available, and it should be straightforward to figure out the dependence between basic blocks (or states).

Parallelization will be beneficial for all angr analyses, not on `VSA_DDG`. In the past few weeks, we worked on creating a unified data-flow analysis framework (called `ForwardAnalysis` right now), and refactored `CFG` as a subclass of `ForwardAnalysis`. The long-term goal is to make all work-list based analyses subclasses of `ForwardAnalysis`, and I think it might be worthwhile and easier to parallelize `ForwardAnalysis`, instead of parallelizing individual analysis.

However, I'm by no means an expert of program analysis. I bet there must be existing papers or implementation on parallel program dependence graph generation. It is greatly appreciated if you can post what you know or heard here so I can learn more :-)

Excellent! Fix lint and I'll hit merge.

btw with respect to the final newline stuff - this is a problem with editors. The expectation for a "well formmated text file" is that it's a sequence of lines, and every line ends with a newline, including the final line. Not all editors actually do this for you though. If the last character of the file isn't \n, lint will complain. Additionally, if the file ends with more than one \n, then lint will also complain, because there are trailing blank lines. So if there's no final newline and you try to fix this by adding a blank line, you might actually have added two newlines, one for the old last line and one for the blank line. It's always tricky to resolve. Vim will give you a [noeol] thing or something as a message when you load a file without the final char being a newline.

> 1.) how do the wheels get built and transferred to PyPI?

Appveyor builds the wheels for each PR, so if you modify ``setup.py`` via a PR then all you have to do is grab those wheels and upload them to PyPi. Wheels are stored under the "Artifacts" tab (example: https://ci.appveyor.com/project/PyWavelets/pywt/build/1.0.138/job/hhb9h644f1ro9048/artifacts) and you can upload them from the PyPi web interface (log in, browse to PyWavelets, and use the "upload" button under the Files tab).

> 2.) Is there a script that uploads the HTML docs to PyPI or do I build them locally and upload the docs manually through the web interface?

If you push the ``v0.4.0`` tag to Github then ReadTheDocs will build the html docs automatically. Docs are never uploaded to PyPi. So nothing to do here.

I think there are two relatively easy solutions:
1.) move `reflect` to be the last element in `Modes.modes` list
or
2.) make _old_modes a dict that translates old names to new

So to confirm, the recommend approach to circular references is to import the referenced (or, related) ObjectType after its needed:

```
# a.py
class A(graphene.ObjectType):
     name = graphene.String()
     to_b = graphene.Field(lambda: B)

from .b import B
```

```
# b.py
from .post_type import Post

class B(graphene.ObjectType):
     name = graphene.String()
     to_b = graphene.Field(lambda: A)

from .a import A
```

```
# schema.py
from .a import A
from .b import B


class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
     get_a = graphene.Field(A)
     get_b = graphene.Field(B)

schema = graphene.Schema(query=Query)
```

The other alternative I'm aware of is the method you mentioned [here](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/issues/110#issuecomment-180631147) using `graphene.LazyType`.

Yes, generally a real FFT is faster than a complex FFT. You can get more speed up by using Float32 if you don't need the full 64 bits of precision.

Powers of 2 definitely speed things up, but then the difference would be less when compared to Numpy (which is actually pretty quick now). You can use the builders to do this automatically if you want - the implementation is pretty optimised (it's all slicing in place) using an FFTW proxy wrapper.

Wisdom is just exported as a tuple of strings, which can be saved as you want (using e.g. pickle).

If you want to squeeze as much performance out as you can you'll need to look at the alignment and also the number of threads used.

It's non obvious how all these various choices interact, so the best option is to test over your various design parameters and see which is fastest (using timeit). I suggest reading _all_ the docs so you understand what's implemented for you - some stuff can use behind the scene automatic optimisations.

```
    def writerow(self, row):
        if self.line_numbers:
            row = list(row)
            self._append_line_number(row)

So I tried things today with joblib and the following example:

```python
from dask.distributed import Client, progress
import distributed.joblib  # needed until sklearn 0.20
client = Client(processes=False)

from skopt import BayesSearchCV
# parameter ranges are specified by one of below
from skopt.space import Real, Categorical, Integer

from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.externals.joblib import parallel_backend

X, y = load_iris(True)
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, train_size=0.75,
                                                    random_state=0)


# log-uniform: understand as search over p = exp(x) by varying x
opt = BayesSearchCV(
    SVC(),
    {
        'C': Real(1e-6, 1e+6, prior='log-uniform'),
        'gamma': Real(1e-6, 1e+1, prior='log-uniform'),
        'degree': Integer(1,8),
        'kernel': Categorical(['linear', 'poly', 'rbf']),
    },
    n_iter=32,
    n_jobs=1,
)


with parallel_backend('dask'):
    # executes bayesian optimization
    opt.fit(X_train, y_train)
```

and the dashboard shows tasks like the following:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/306380/42777271-82d4a9de-8907-11e8-9cfa-99aa596f498e.png)

Each green rectangle is a call to `fmin_l_bfgs_batch`, which takes around 50ms.  We're seeing a lot of dead space in between tasks, likely meaning that `skopt` is doing some non-trivial work in between sending out batches.  

After running this experiment I have some questions:

1.  Is scikit-optimize simple enough that just using joblib is the right way to go?  Or are there things that we would want to do with a more expressive interface
2.  In particular, I might suggest trying to solve this problem with the concurrent.futures interface.  You would submit lots of tasks (presumably a bit more than you had cores), then as they completed you would update your next best guess and submit that one out to run with the others.  This is maybe a similar problem to what is running today, you just have more tasks in flight.
3.  Is there a problem with a larger cost so that we can see how much the overhead hurts in a realistic setting?
4.  What is the cause of the overhead here?  

    Actually I profiled a bit.  It's spending a bit of time in scipy.optimize, coming from the `skopt/optimizer/optimizer.py::Optimizer._tell` method.

That's a dask array holding scipy sparse matricies in each block. It can be converted to a Dask holding pydata/sparse matricies with


```python

from sparse import COO

trn.map_blocks(COO.from_scipy_sparse, dtype=trn.dtype)

We had a meeting about the Certbot command line awhile ago and decided we should make some changes here. In general, we decided that we should favor add more verbs/subcommands (which can be nicely grouped in our help as shown in #3883) rather than more flags to change behaviors of existing subcommands.

What this means here is that:

* `certbot register --update-registration` should become it's own subcommand. I'm open to suggestions on the name here. To implement this, we can probably change the verb in `certbot.cli.HelpfulArgumentParser.parse_args` if `certbot register --update-registration` is used to preserve compatibility.
* `certbot register --deactivate` as implemented in #3571 should become something like `certbot unregister`.
* `certbot register` should only be used to create a new account with the ACME CA. We can potentially change the name of this subcommand but I personally like it given its new, more specific purpose.

I'm on your side and would say that intuition is the key here and regardless if one would branch on behaviour at the API:s top-level (i.e. two separate declarations -> `PostGenerationMethodCall` and `PostGenerationX`) or "under the hood" configurable through options to `PostGenerationMethodCall`/`@post_generation`, it would have to become clear what usage would fit what situation.

At the top of my head, I'm imagining the following use cases, where we can pan out differently:

(1.)
When the intention is to mutate the method/function being called post generation we might have the (generated) object acting with a function based API where the object is passed as argument. We can say that we're working with `n` callback methods/functions, on post generation the object is to go through 1 of them (to make it a simpler case).

(2.)
When the intention is to mutate the argument(s)/keywords to a _set_ method/function being called post generation we might have an API acting on an input argument together with the (generated) object. We can say that we're working with `1` callback method/function, on post generation it can be called with an argument of `n` (different) values.

Some example code: (note that I'm trying to give 2 separate implementation approaches supporting/enabling identical functionality)

```python
# Example Case 1.

class MyObjWithState(models.Model):
    def preprocess(self, *args, **kwargs):
        # Do stuff

    def accept(self, *args, **kwargs):
        # Do stuff

    def decline(self, *args, **kwargs):
        # Do stuff


class MyObjWithStateFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory):
    state = factory.PostGenerationMethodCall('preprocess')

    class Meta:
        model = MyObjWithState

    class Params:
        accepted = factory.Trait(state=factory.PostGenerationMethodCall('accept'))
        declined = factory.Trait(state=factory.PostGenerationMethodCall('decline'))
```

```python
# Example Case 2.

class MyObjWithState(models.Model):
    def set_state(self, *args, **kwargs):
        # Do stuff


class MyObjWithStateFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory):
    state = factory.PostGenerationMethodCall('set_state', 'preprocessed')

    class Meta:
        model = MyObjWithState

    class Params:
        accepted = factory.Trait(state=factory.PostGenerationMethodCall('set_state', 'accepted'))
        declined = factory.Trait(state=factory.PostGenerationMethodCall('set_state', 'declined'))
```

Unfortunately, as we've concluded in previous comments, a user with approach Case 2 would have to suffer a bit with his implementation, although it looks completely fine for Case 1.

I personally don't have any opinion if one would have to write(apart from that this could clash badly with kwargs to `set_state`) e.g.:
```python
state = factory.PostGenerationMethodCall('set_state', 'preprocessed', options={'x': x})
```
It could look fine with `@post_generation`:
```python
@factory.post_generation(options={'x': x})
def state(*args, **kwargs):
    pass
```
As long as this would allow:
> 3. If you pass (when calling or with a `SubFactory`) a scalar value or a non-`PostGeneration` declaration, it **gets passed as an argument** to your method

e.g.
```python
class Params:
    accepted = factory.Trait(state='accepted')
    declined = factory.Trait(state='declined')
```

The best scenario might even be, for intuition, to add a separate declaration for when passing a non-`PostGeneration` declaration:

```python
factory.PostGenerationMethodInput(method_name='set_state', *args, **kwargs)
```
(I find the naming here to be quite difficult, `PostGenerationMethodInput` is just an example)

What do you think, are we abusing e.g. `PostGenerationMethodCall` when trying to reuse it for multiple approaches like this?
