@joneswong I just finished an ablation test run on rainbow on {qbert, spaceinvaders, breakout, beamrider}. The sample size is small, but there seem to be some patterns:

I performed both leave-one-out ablation and single optimization runs. The raw data is here: https://github.com/ray-project/rl-experiments/tree/rainbow-ablation/atari-dqn-ablation

For each optimization, I looked at the relative benefit compared to other optimizations. I used the same hyperparameters for all experiments (and for the no-noisy case, annealed to 0.01 eps in 200k timesteps).

```
Leave-one-out ablation relative benefit:
atari-dqn-ablation/no-{beamrider, breakout, spaceinvaders, qbert}
    duel:    +2 +2 +3 +3  =  +10  (possible bug in dueling nets?)
    noisy:   +3 +3 -1 +2  =  +7  (possible bug in noisy nets?)
    double:  +1 -1 +1 -1  =  +0
    distrib: -2 -3 +2 +1  =  -2
    prio:    -1 +1 -2 -2  =  -4
    nstep:   -3 -2 -3 -3  =  -11

Just one optimization relative benefit:
atari-dqn-ablation/just-{beamrider, breakout, spaceinvaders, qbert}
    distrib: +3 +3 +3 +2  = +11
    duel:    +2 +1 +2 +1  = +6
    nstep:   +1 -2 -1 +3  = +1
    double:  -1 +2 -2 -1  = -2
    noisy:   -2 -1 +1 -3  = -5 (possible bug in noisy nets?)
    prio:    -3 -3 -3 -2  = -11 (possible bug in prioritization?)

Combined relative benefit:
    distrib: +11 - -2   =  +13
    nstep:   +1  - -11  =  +12
    double:  -2  - +0   =  -2
    duel:    +6  - +10  =  -4
    prio:    -11 - -4   =  -7 (possible bug in prioritization?)
    noisy:   -5  - +7   =  -12 (likely bug in noisy nets?)
```

Overall it seems to point to some issue in the noisy net implementation, which seems to hurt in most cases. On the other hand, we can be confident that n-step and distributional DQN are probably correct!

There also might be some issue with prioritization, though I've looked at the code a couple times and I think we're applying the IS weights correctly.

`client.join(output, timeout=60)`

`timeout` on `run_command` is for the individual host stdout/stderr channels (see [docs](http://parallel-ssh.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pssh2_client.html#pssh.pssh2_client.ParallelSSHClient.run_command)).

`join` timeout is a per-host wait time which, if exceeded, the `join` call will raise `Timeout` exception along with which host the timeout occurred on. If the script should not wait for more than N seconds in total for all hosts then join timeout can be set to `N/len(client.hosts)`.

This of course assuming the commands are hanging on execution and not connection/authentication (ie, `run_command` has returned). Connection/authentication timeouts will need to be set prior to running `run_command` with `ParallelSSHClient(timeout=<..>)` and/or with socket/OS level timeout settings.

Here's what I propose : 

The DFK will have two methods:
1. checkpoint( ) - write all non-checkpointed info to a checkpoint object
2. resume_from( ) that loads all checkpointed information from a checkpoint object

We will support three modes for automatic checkpointing:
1.  (eager) checkpoint at the completion of every task or 
2.  (lazy) at configurable intervals (say ~1hr).
3.  (at_exit) write out a checkpoint when the DFK exits, capturing early termination from app failures,
uncaught exceptions, keyboard interrupts etc.

In the simplest case, code does not change, functions don't hold any state (no clojures) and are deterministic. So if an app executes a function named app_foo( ), every subsequent invocation with the same args, kwargs and env would produce the same results. With this assumption, if we store completed results and return these results at call time as completed futures we would skip every invocation of a task that has been completed as we re-create the dataflow-graph on re-execution.

Complications: 
* Tracking code change, and forcing re-execution of parts of the graph.
* Function could be redefined in different contexts
* Data objects could be very deep or not comparable
* Changes to file objects aren't trackable since they are right now treated as strings, this would change once File objects are used consistently, but would require means to compare files.
* Non-determinism from the script itself via conditions

I think we can start with the case which required explicitly passing all variables and only works with pickleable parameters. The general idea would be this - if in the cell the function call looks like:
`x = f(a,b,c)` we'll create a wrapper script that pickles the arguments a,b,c (maybe f as well - need to look into how python serialization works with functions) and passes them as artifacts; on the worker side, it will get artifacts, unpickle data and call f; then pickle the return value and pass it back as an artifact. Then later we can add globals/locals as variables. 
Fortunately, turns out that we already have most of this pickle-arguments - call - pickle return values logic implemented  in @jasonzliang's completion service (the name is still open to debate, but functionality is essentially exactly as described); so we can build on that.

Hello,

the problem you are facing is the one we haven't completely solved it ourselves. For now we don't know how to correctly decode `jp2` data without first saving them.

So the quick solution is to first save data with either `request.save_data()` method or `request.get_data(save_data=True)` method. You might also want to leave `redownload` flag to `False`. That will allow you that next time you call `request.get_data()` the data will be read from disk and not downloaded again. So this should work:
```Python
from sentinelhub.data_request import AwsProductRequest
product_id = 'S2A_MSIL1C_20171010T003621_N0205_R002_T01WCV_20171010T003615'
data_folder = 'C:/path/to/folder'

request = AwsProductRequest(product_id=product_id, data_folder=data_folder)
data_list = request.get_data(save_data=True)
```
About the folder path I believe it should work if you specify either relative or absolute path.
